title: Which-dictionary routing — gold panel
purpose: Independent expected-answer panel for the GH-1 routing-accuracy hypothesis (see /about/guides-hypotheses). Each scenario carries the dictionary a lexicographer would route the user to, judged against the dictionaries' own front matter and the csl-atlas OBS evidence — not copied from the quiz key.
annotator: Fable 5 (claude-fable-5), single pass, 2026-07-07; no second annotator yet (known limitation, recorded in the hypothesis note).
grounding: Dictionary identities and years from src/data/dictionaries.json (generated from live CDSL sources); novelty context from the vendored csl-atlas extract src/data/atlas-extract.json.

scenarios.scenario: Broadest classical Sanskrit→English reference that also marks Vedic accents.
scenarios.rationale: MW (1899) is the encyclopedic general Sanskrit–English reference and prints Vedic accents; PWG is excluded by the English requirement.

scenarios.scenario: A Sanskritized Pali-looking form in the Mahāvastu, absent from MW.
scenarios.rationale: Edgerton's Buddhist Hybrid Sanskrit Dictionary (1953) is the only CDSL dictionary indexing the BHS canon.

scenarios.scenario: Deepest scholarly treatment with fullest citations; German acceptable.
scenarios.rationale: The Grosses Petersburger Wörterbuch (1855–75) remains the fullest citation apparatus in the corpus (csl-atlas OBS-C: highest locator-bearing <ls> volume).

scenarios.scenario: Clean numbered senses and translated literary quotations for classical (non-Vedic) reading.
scenarios.rationale: Apte 1890 matches exactly; the 1957 revised edition (AP) has the same design and is a defensible alternate.

scenarios.scenario: English word → Sanskrit equivalents (composition).
scenarios.rationale: Apte's Student's English–Sanskrit (1920) is the standard recommendation; MWE (1851) and BOR (1877) are the other English→Sanskrit dictionaries in CDSL.

scenarios.scenario: A modern French gloss.
scenarios.rationale: Stchoupak–Nitti–Renou (1932) is the modern French dictionary; Burnouf (1866) is not modern.

scenarios.scenario: Latin glosses and Indo-European cognates by the founder of comparative grammar.
scenarios.rationale: Bopp's Glossarium Sanscritum (1847) is the only Sanskrit–Latin dictionary in CDSL.

scenarios.scenario: Identify a Sanskrit plant name botanically.
scenarios.rationale: Meulenbeld's Sanskrit Names of Plants (1974) is the botanical identification resource.

scenarios.scenario: Administrative/fiscal term in an inscription, technical sense needed.
scenarios.rationale: Sircar's Indian Epigraphical Glossary (1966) covers inscriptional technical vocabulary.

scenarios.scenario: Identify and locate a proper name in the Mahābhārata.
scenarios.rationale: Sörensen's Index (1904) locates MBh names; the Mahabharata Cultural Index (1993) is an alternate for cultural realia.

scenarios.scenario: The story and identity behind a Purāṇic name, in clear English.
scenarios.rationale: Mani's Puranic Encyclopedia (1975) narrates; Dikshitar's Purana Index (1951) only locates references.

scenarios.scenario: Classical synonyms of a word, in the traditional kośa form.
scenarios.rationale: Any of the digitized synonymic kośas answers this; ABCH (Hemacandra) is the fullest of them in CDSL, with ARMH and NMMB defensible alternates.

scenarios.scenario: A name or subject in Vedic literature, with scholarly references.
scenarios.rationale: Macdonell–Keith's Vedic Index (1912) is the Vedic onomasticon.

scenarios.scenario: A root's grammatical profile — class, seṭ/aniṭ, pada, kṛt derivatives.
scenarios.rationale: Kṛdantarūpamālā (1965) is the Pāṇinian root reference in CDSL.

scenarios.scenario: The earliest major Sanskrit–English dictionary, MW's foundation.
scenarios.rationale: Wilson (1819; CDSL digitizes the 1832 second edition) precedes all other Sanskrit–English dictionaries in the corpus.

scenarios.scenario: Compare Monier-Williams 1872 vs 1899 treatment of a word.
scenarios.rationale: MW72 and MW are digitized as distinct texts; the comparison needs both codes.

scenarios.scenario: A compact, accented student's dictionary for reading.
scenarios.rationale: Macdonell (1893) is the canonical accented student's dictionary; Cappeller's Sanskrit–English (1891) is a defensible compact alternate.

scenarios.scenario: A word/sense missing from PW; where are the scholarly additions?
scenarios.rationale: Schmidt's Nachträge (1928) supplements PW, keyed to its numbered senses.

scenarios.scenario: You want the definition IN Sanskrit, with śāstric quotations — a Sanskrit–Sanskrit encyclopedic lexicon.
scenarios.rationale: Śabdakalpadruma (37.1% unique headwords per csl-atlas OBS-R) and Vācaspatyam are the indigenous encyclopedic lexica; neither is ever a quiz routing target.

scenarios.scenario: You met a Sanskrit work title or author name and want to know where the work is attested and catalogued.
scenarios.rationale: Aufrecht's Catalogus Catalogorum — the highest dictionary-unique headword share in the corpus (43.3%, csl-atlas OBS-R) — appears only as a distractor (WD-10), never as an answer.

scenarios.scenario: MW marks a word 'L.' (lexicographers only) and you want to verify it in the indigenous lexica it came from.
scenarios.rationale: The 'L.' verification path leads to the Sanskrit–Sanskrit kośas and encyclopedic lexica — a recurring MW-reader task the routing quiz does not teach.

scenarios.scenario: A Ṝgvedic word: you want its accent and every verse location, grouped by construction/meaning.
scenarios.rationale: Grassmann's Wörterbuch zum Rig Veda (1873) is the concordance-dictionary for exactly this; it is never a routing target (VEI covers names/subjects, not lexical concordance).

scenarios.scenario: A beginner reading a graded reader wants the reader's own vocabulary, not a full dictionary.
scenarios.rationale: Lanman's Reader vocabulary (1884) and Friš's Reader vocabulary (1956) exist for this; the quiz never routes to a reader vocabulary.

scenarios.scenario: You read German and want a concise one-volume dictionary rather than the seven-volume PWG.
scenarios.rationale: Böhtlingk's kürzere Fassung (1879) and Cappeller's German dictionary (1887) are the compact German options; WD-03 routes only to PWG.
